ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for duck noodles dry style; mapped to broader noodle dish class FOODON_03315595.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (noodle-based dish with meat) used as closest defensible analogue; no specific duck noodle code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of dry duck noodles weighs approximately 350 g, comprising egg noodles, shredded braised duck meat and skin, green vegetables, and a side of black gravy sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Duck Noodles (Dry) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Duck Noodles (Dry), CR ID F05060045. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this exact entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Egg noodles, boiled — GI value 58 (analogous estimate for refined egg noodle base)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Duck Noodles (Dry) as a composite dish. GI of 58 (medium) is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled egg noodles, which form the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 53.0) / 100 ≈ 30.8, based on 350 g serving providing approximately 53.0 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 412 mg/100 g from soy sauce and braising liquid, with negligible potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g, request reduced soy sauce, and avoid adding additional condiments at the table, as a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,442 mg sodium — exceeding 60% of the 2,400 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined egg noodles with medium GI (58) and low dietary fibre (0.86 g/100 g) causing moderate postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the noodle portion and increase the duck meat and vegetable components to lower the glycaemic load, and consider requesting wholemeal or lower-GI noodle alternatives where available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (412 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (6.54 g/100 g) including duck skin contributing saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to small portions, remove duck skin before eating to reduce saturated fat intake, and request reduced-sodium preparation to manage blood pressure and arterial load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 412 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold, and refined noodle base contributes to glycaemic load-driven inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced soy sauce to lower sodium, add leafy greens or ginger to introduce anti-inflammatory components, and keep portions small to minimise inflammatory dietary load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Duck Noodles (Dry) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of springy egg noodles tossed in a dark, savoury black soy sauce gravy, topped with shredded braised duck meat and skin, and served alongside blanched green vegetables. The 'dry' style distinguishes it from the soup version: the noodles are drained and coated in a concentrated braising sauce rather than served in broth, producing a richer, more intensely flavoured dish. The duck is slow-braised in a five-spice and soy-based master stock until tender, yielding moist, flavourful meat with a slightly gelatinous skin. Per 100 g, the dish provides 148 kcal, 7.3 g protein, 6.5 g fat, and 15.2 g carbohydrate, with a moderate sodium content of 412 mg. Dietary fibre is low at 0.86 g/100 g. The egg noodle base has a medium glycaemic index of approximately 58, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The high sodium content from soy sauce and braising liquid is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and contains gluten, egg, and soy allergens. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal at hawker centres and coffee shops across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in duck noodles dry?
Is duck noodles dry suitable for diabetics?
Sodium content of duck noodles Singapore
Duck noodles dry vs soup which is healthier?
Can I eat duck noodles with high blood pressure?
What is gan lao ya rou mian?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact match entry (CR ID F05060045). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled egg noodles as the primary carbohydrate component. All mandatory multilingual names are present. Ontology uses defensible broader/analogous codes with documented gap notes.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Duck Noodles (Dry) (F05060045) does not report detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrients, or moisture data. These values are not available in the HPB source as of 2026-04-16. Input cultural tags ('western', 'vegetarian', 'plant_based', 'low_calorie') were assessed as incorrect for this dish and have been corrected: cuisine is Singaporean Chinese, dietary flags reflect duck meat content (not vegetarian/vegan). Glycemic load per serving recalculated using 350 g standard serving size: GL = (58 × 53.025) / 100 ≈ 30.8.
